# AI-MIT License

**A permissive open-source license for code generated by AI agents — with or without human involvement.**

> *This license was itself written by an AI (Claude, Anthropic) at the direction of a human author. We think that's fitting.*

[![License: AI-MIT-1.0](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-AI--MIT--1.0-4A90D9.svg)](./LICENSE)
[![Status: Proposal](https://img.shields.io/badge/status-community%20proposal-yellow.svg)]()
[![PRs Welcome](https://img.shields.io/badge/PRs-welcome-brightgreen.svg)]()

---

## The Problem

Every existing open-source license was designed with a human author in mind.

The MIT License begins with `Copyright (c) [year] [author]`. But what happens when there is no human author — or when the "author" is an autonomous AI agent running inside a CI/CD pipeline at 3am?

Today's reality:

- **AI agents** generate entire codebases autonomously, without a human writing a single line.
- **Copyright law** in most jurisdictions explicitly requires human authorship — meaning fully AI-generated code may belong to *no one*, or its ownership is simply undefined.
- **Existing licenses** applied to AI-generated code create a false impression: they imply human authorship and copyright claims that may be legally void.
- **Training data provenance** is opaque — AI-generated code may carry embedded fragments of third-party code from training, which no standard license addresses.

The open-source community deserves a license that is honest about these realities.

---

## The Solution

**AI-MIT License** takes the simplest, most widely adopted open-source license (MIT) and adapts it for the AI age with three targeted changes:

| What we kept | What we changed |
|---|---|
| Maximum permissiveness | Added an **Authorship Declaration** (3 modes) |
| Use / copy / modify / sell freely | Added **Transparency** condition for AI-origin disclosure |
| Standard AS IS disclaimer | Added **AI-specific disclaimers** (training data, regulations) |
| Attribution requirement | Added **No Copyright Claim** clause for fully autonomous code |

The result is a license that is still one page, still human-readable, and still as permissive as MIT — but honest.

---

## The Three Authorship Modes

The license requires the distributor to declare one of three modes:

```
[ ] Fully AI-generated   — no human creative input; no copyright claimed
[ ] AI-assisted          — human-directed, AI-generated output  
[ ] Human-authored       — AI used only as a tool (e.g. autocomplete)
```

This single checkbox determines the copyright posture of the entire repository.

---

## Quick Start

### 1. Copy the LICENSE file into your repository root

Fill in the Authorship Declaration at the top:

```
  [x] AI-assisted

  Originator : Acme Corp
  AI Tool(s) : Claude Sonnet 4 (Anthropic, 2025)
  Year       : 2025
  Repository : https://github.com/acme/my-project
```

### 2. Add a header to your source files (optional but recommended)

```python
# SPDX-License-Identifier: AI-MIT-1.0
# Authorship: AI-assisted
# AI Tool: Claude Sonnet 4 (Anthropic, 2025)
# Originator: Acme Corp
```

### 3. For fully autonomous agents

If an AI agent generated the code with no human creative input, mark `Fully AI-generated`. No copyright is claimed — the code is effectively public domain. Add the agent's name or the system that ran it as the Originator.

```
  [x] Fully AI-generated

  Originator : DeployBot v2 / Acme Corp infrastructure
  AI Tool(s) : Claude Sonnet 4 (Anthropic, 2025)
  Year       : 2025
  Repository : https://github.com/acme/generated-api-client
```

---

## Legal Context (as of 2025–2026)

This license is designed as a pragmatic tool in a period of legal uncertainty:

- **USA**: The US Copyright Office (January 2025) confirmed that a prompt alone does not create copyright. Autonomously generated code exists in a legal grey zone. Litigation is ongoing.
- **EU**: The EU AI Act requires transparency about AI-generated content. Copyright Directive does not clearly address AI authorship.
- **Russia**: Civil Code requires a human author for copyright to exist. Autonomous AI code is formally unprotected.
- **China**: Beijing Internet Court (2023) recognized copyright when a human made substantial creative contributions to AI output.
- **UK**: Uniquely, UK copyright law allows protection for "computer-generated works" with no human author (CDPA 1988, s.9(3)).

> **This license does not constitute legal advice.** For commercial or high-stakes deployments, consult a qualified IP attorney in your jurisdiction.

---

## Why Not Just Use Unlicense or CC0?

Good question. Some projects already use [Unlicense](https://unlicense.org/) or [CC0](https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/) for AI-generated code, dedicating it to the public domain.

AI-MIT differs in two important ways:

1. **It handles mixed authorship.** Unlicense/CC0 make no distinction between fully autonomous code and AI-assisted human work. AI-MIT has three modes, each with the appropriate legal posture.

2. **It requires transparency.** If you use AI-MIT, you must disclose that the code is AI-generated and name the tool. This matters for supply-chain security, regulatory compliance, and intellectual honesty.

---

## FAQ

**Q: Do I need permission from MIT to use this license?**  
No. "MIT License" is just a historical name — the Massachusetts Institute of Technology has no ownership or control over it as a legal template. AI-MIT is an independent new license that is inspired by MIT's structure and philosophy.

**Q: Is this OSI-approved?**  
Not yet. This is a community proposal. We intend to submit it to the OSI license review process. Feedback and contributions are welcome and will strengthen the submission.

**Q: Can I use this for proprietary projects?**  
The license itself is permissive — there are no copyleft conditions. However, the Authorship Declaration is a disclosure requirement, not a restriction on use.

**Q: What if I don't know which AI tools were used?**  
Be as specific as you can. If the tools are unknown (e.g., inherited codebase), write "AI Tool(s): Unknown" and note the uncertainty. Honesty is the point.

**Q: What happens if copyright law catches up and AI-generated code becomes copyrightable?**  
The license is forward-compatible. The "No Copyright Claim" clause applies "to the maximum extent permitted by applicable law" — if law changes, the clause narrows accordingly. The Human-authored and AI-assisted modes already support full copyright.

## Contributing

This is a living document. We welcome:

- **Legal review** — especially from practitioners in non-US jurisdictions
- **Translations** — see `/translations/` folder
- **Edge cases** — open an issue describing a scenario the license handles poorly
- **Tooling** — scripts, GitHub Actions, IDE plugins

Please read [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md) before opening a PR.

## License of This Repository

The AI-MIT License text itself (the `LICENSE` file) is released into the **public domain** — no restrictions, no attribution required. Copy it, adapt it, use it.

All other files in this repository (README, translations, tooling) are released under AI-MIT-1.0 with the following declaration:

```
Authorship : AI-assisted
Originator : Community contributors (see git log)
AI Tool(s) : Claude Sonnet 4 (Anthropic, 2025)
Year       : 2025
```

---

*"The question of who owns a thing built by a machine is one of the defining legal questions of our time. We might as well start answering it honestly."*
